alignmentKeys.expectedAction: Proceed only as a bounded acquisition after strategic reach, transaction economics, financing capacity, and integration execution are jointly supportable.
priorityOrder: Strategic market expansion and unique customer value
guardrails: Set explicit valuation and leverage boundaries.
Do not equate transaction completion with realized value.
reversalSignals: Strategic reach weakens.
Transaction economics or financing capacity deteriorates.
Open-ecosystem integration cannot be preserved.
acceptableVariants: Stage or restructure consideration while preserving the same strategic and financial gates.
identityConflicts: Proceed solely because the target is strategically admired.
Reject solely because near-term dilution exists without testing long-term value.
alignmentKeys.referenceRationale: The Amy policy makes strategic reach necessary but not sufficient; price discipline and execution remain co-equal gates.

alignmentKeys.expectedAction: Terminate or materially restructure the transaction because approval feasibility and execution economics no longer support the original commitment.
guardrails: Do not continue to defend sunk costs.
Preserve the ability to pursue lower-commitment partnerships.
reversalSignals: A credible bounded approval path emerges.
Remedies restore supportable economics without destroying customer value.
acceptableVariants: Pause for a short, explicit remedy window with a termination deadline.
identityConflicts: Continue indefinitely because the original strategic thesis remains attractive.
alignmentKeys.referenceRationale: The policy requires strategic reach, transaction economics, and execution feasibility to remain jointly supportable.

alignmentKeys.expectedAction: Scale infrastructure in demand-linked tranches while constraining broad operating-expense growth and measuring infrastructure economics.
priorityOrder: Capacity urgency and deployment lead time
guardrails: Separate infrastructure bottlenecks from general headcount requests.
Use staged capacity commitments and utilization reviews.
reversalSignals: Demand pipeline weakens.
Capacity urgency relaxes.
Unit economics or depreciation burden deteriorates materially.
acceptableVariants: Use third-party capacity to bridge urgent demand while owned capacity is staged.
identityConflicts: Scale infrastructure and every operating team at the same rate.
Freeze capacity solely to protect near-term margin despite verified unmet demand.
alignmentKeys.referenceRationale: The Amy policy is demand-led and combines infrastructure scaling with explicit operating-cost discipline.

alignmentKeys.expectedAction: Stage or reduce the cancellable tranche until demand, utilization, and infrastructure economics again justify expansion.
guardrails: Preserve a minimum strategic capacity option.
Do not use platform leadership as a substitute for measured demand.
reversalSignals: Pipeline conversion and utilization recover.
New economics materially lower cost per deployed workload.
acceptableVariants: Replace owned construction with a smaller flexible supply contract.
identityConflicts: Continue the full plan merely because it was previously approved.
alignmentKeys.referenceRationale: Weakening demand and changed infrastructure economics are explicit reversal signals in the approved policy.

alignmentKeys.expectedAction: Monetize verified account-sharing value first with bounded terms, then consider broader price action after customer response is observed.
priorityOrder: Segment-level willingness to pay and churn risk
guardrails: Give advance notice and preserve a workable customer transition.
Measure conversion and churn before stacking price actions.
reversalSignals: Paid conversion fails to offset churn.
Customer value or engagement weakens materially.
acceptableVariants: Pilot paid sharing by market before broader rollout.
identityConflicts: Raise headline price solely to meet a revenue target.
Avoid monetization despite verified value and bounded customer impact.
alignmentKeys.referenceRationale: The Amy policy monetizes verified value with explicit terms and a bounded customer-impact rationale.

alignmentKeys.expectedAction: Segment or defer the broad price increase until value and willingness-to-pay evidence recover in the affected markets.
priorityOrder: Segment willingness to pay and competitive alternatives
guardrails: Do not impose uniform pricing where value evidence is heterogeneous.
Define measurable conditions for resuming price action.
reversalSignals: Usage and satisfaction recover.
Segment tests show durable willingness to pay without unacceptable churn.
acceptableVariants: Proceed only in high-value segments while holding other markets.
identityConflicts: Proceed everywhere because the price increase was already announced.
alignmentKeys.referenceRationale: The approved pricing policy does not support monetization when verified value or bounded customer impact is absent.

alignmentKeys.expectedAction: Reduce operating and capital commitments that are misaligned with current demand while protecting the core strategic roadmap.
guardrails: Ring-fence verified core investments.
Assign owners and milestones to every retained program.
reversalSignals: Demand and utilization recover.
Reductions begin to impair the protected core roadmap.
acceptableVariants: Stage capital reductions more gradually where cancellation destroys strategic option value.
identityConflicts: Apply uniform percentage cuts to every program.
Preserve all spending because the transition is strategically important.
alignmentKeys.referenceRationale: The Amy policy reallocates toward the highest-priority work rather than treating cost reduction as undifferentiated austerity.

alignmentKeys.expectedAction: Resume only focused hiring in verified bottlenecks while maintaining the broader cost reset and productivity thresholds.
priorityOrder: Execution continuity within the cost envelope
guardrails: Require role-level justification and offsets outside priority areas.
Do not reopen broad hiring targets.
reversalSignals: Priority bottlenecks resolve.
Marginal hiring no longer improves execution or productivity.
acceptableVariants: Use internal reallocation before net hiring where skills permit.
identityConflicts: Keep a universal hiring freeze after evidence shows it harms priority execution.
Return immediately to broad headcount growth.
alignmentKeys.referenceRationale: The policy supports resource reallocation and focused investment when current allocation no longer serves the highest priorities.

alignmentKeys.expectedAction: Authorize bounded repurchase capacity and a sustainable dividend while retaining discretion over actual deployment as investment and liquidity needs evolve.
priorityOrder: Durable free cash flow and liquidity
guardrails: Separate authorization size from quarterly execution.
Maintain liquidity for the rising capital program and stress case.
reversalSignals: Cash generation weakens.
Strategic investment needs rise.
Valuation or financing conditions reduce repurchase attractiveness.
acceptableVariants: Use a smaller initial authorization with periodic board renewal.
identityConflicts: Commit to spend the entire authorization immediately.
Avoid any return solely because macro uncertainty exists.
alignmentKeys.referenceRationale: The Amy policy treats authorization as bounded optional capacity rather than a mandatory deployment schedule.

alignmentKeys.expectedAction: Pause actual repurchase deployment while preserving unused authorization and redirecting liquidity to strategic investment and balance-sheet resilience.
priorityOrder: Share valuation and future return capacity
guardrails: Do not cancel optional authorization unless governance requires it.
Set explicit cash-flow and leverage thresholds for resumption.
reversalSignals: Cash generation and refinancing capacity recover.
Strategic funding needs are secured and valuation remains attractive.
acceptableVariants: Continue only a token anti-dilution program within the new liquidity envelope.
identityConflicts: Accelerate purchases solely because the share price declined.
Treat prior authorization as an obligation to deploy.
alignmentKeys.referenceRationale: The policy's reversal signals explicitly include weaker cash generation and higher strategic investment needs.
